title: Four Courses in Two Days on the Alabama Gulf Coast
body:
Gulf Shores, Alabama wasn't on our radar. None of us had been. When Tee Times USA pitched us the idea of a quick two-day trip down to the Gulf Coast to play four of the area's better courses, we said yes mostly because we've never said no to a golf trip in our lives. We had no expectations going in. Then we played the first round, and the second, and the third, and by the time we walked off the 18th green at Craft Farms on Saturday afternoon, the question wasn't whether we'd come back. It was when.

This is one of the easiest buddies trip recommendations we can make. Two-hour direct flight from a lot of the country. A beachfront condo for the group. Four really different courses inside a 45-minute radius. You don't lose half your trip to driving, and you don't have to dress up to play any of them. It's pure golf and a balcony with an ocean view.

Tee Times USA ran the whole thing the way you want a trip to run. Tee times locked, condo booked, vouchers in hand, four reservations across two days that we didn't have to think about once. If you're trying to put a group of four together and you don't want to be the guy spreadsheet-managing it, just call them. The whole package was one phone call.

## The Setup

We flew into Pensacola and drove about an hour west into Orange Beach. Home base was a 3-bedroom condo at Summer House on Romar Beach, booked through Vacasa. Gulf-front, top floor, big balcony, kitchen, pool, hot tub. Slept four guys comfortably with room to spread out. The view was hit or miss. It was foggy most mornings and we couldn't see much past the balcony rail, but the unit did the job.

The geography here is the underrated part. Lakewood is up near Point Clear, an hour and change northwest. Kiva Dunes is out on the tip of the Fort Morgan peninsula. Peninsula Golf and Racquet is in the same general neighborhood. Craft Farms is right in Gulf Shores proper. Everything is within an hour of the condo, most things much less. You're never burning a half day in the car trying to get somewhere.

We packed two days hard. Friday: Lakewood in the morning, Peninsula in the afternoon. Saturday: Kiva Dunes in the morning, Craft Farms in the afternoon. Four rounds in two days is a workload. By Saturday evening we were toast. It worked, but if you can swing it, plan for three days instead. Same four rounds, more breathing room between them.

## Round 1: Lakewood Club – Azalea Course

![Lakewood Club Azalea Course](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_3.27.22_PM_qkepr6.png)

First tee time of the trip was 7:30 on Friday morning at the Lakewood Club, which sits on the Robert Trent Jones Golf Trail up in Point Clear. The RTJ Trail in Alabama is one of the cooler golf-trail concepts in the country. It's a network of really well-conditioned public courses scattered across the state, all run to the same standard. Lakewood was our introduction to it, and it's a great one.

The course itself is the Azalea, one of two 18s at Lakewood. Originally laid out by Perry Maxwell, the same guy who designed Southern Hills in Tulsa, where they hosted the 2001 U.S. Open. You can feel the pedigree walking it. Big oaks, spring-fed lakes, fairways that roll and bend through mature trees. It's a course where you're not just standing up and bombing driver every hole. You're picking your spots. Lots of doglegs. Lots of holes where the smart play is two clubs less off the tee than you want to hit.

The greens were lightning fast. It felt like you were putting on glass. I missed the first putt of the round by ten feet.

The course also has these little fox squirrels that are kind of their thing. They're on the logo, on the hats, all over the property. Unique-looking squirrels. You'll see them.

By the time we made the turn the sun was up, the course had cleared out, and we'd settled in. The Azalea is a great opener for a trip like this. Not so hard that you spiral on day one. Not so easy that it feels like a warmup. Just a really solid track on really good turf.

## Round 2: Peninsula Golf & Racquet Club (Lakes + Cypress)

![Peninsula Golf Club](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_3.09.46_PM_hvac9u.png)

We grabbed a quick lunch, drove an hour back south, and teed off at Peninsula at 1:45. Peninsula sits on the Fort Morgan peninsula between Mobile Bay and the Gulf, and it's a 27-hole property with three different nines. We played the Lakes nine and the Cypress nine.

The combination of those two nines is where this course earns its reputation. Holes 7 and 8 on the Lakes are the ones you'll remember. You come around a corner and the water just opens up in front of you. We were standing on the seventh tee trying to figure out whether we were looking at the Gulf or Mobile Bay (you can see Mobile Bay from parts of the course, hence the name), and frankly we never settled the debate. What we did settle: those two holes alone justify the round. A long dogleg-right par five with the water as a backdrop, followed by a scorable par three with the same view. Back to back. The kind of stretch you take a phone out for.

Cypress had a moment too. Midway through the back, your cart path heads off into the woods on a random wooden boardwalk. The guy who drove our Uber from the airport had mentioned it the day before. Something about "if you see a wooden path on one of the courses, just take it." We had no idea which course or which hole, and then there it was. We took it. It's a sweet little detour through some marsh and tree cover that pops you back out onto the next tee.

We finished at golden hour. The course had been getting progressively better as the round went on, and by the time we walked off 18 with the sun low on the water, we'd made up our minds. Peninsula is a course you want a late tee time at. The whole property turns gold in the last ninety minutes of light.

## Round 3: Kiva Dunes

![Kiva Dunes](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_3.10.38_PM_yrotjo.png)

Saturday morning, 7:28 tee time at Kiva Dunes. This was the course we'd heard the most about before the trip. Everyone we'd talked to who'd played golf in Alabama said the same thing: if you go to Gulf Shores, you have to play Kiva.

They were right.

Kiva is the most unique course we played on the trip, hands down. It's a links-style course built into actual dunes on a secluded piece of land out near the tip of the peninsula. It doesn't feel like Alabama. You're walking up over a dune to a tee box and seeing nothing but sand, fescue, and sky in every direction.

Every hole at Kiva is different. There's nothing repetitive about the routing. You're getting links holes, dune holes, holes where you can't see the green off the tee, holes that drop down into bowls of fescue and pop you back out onto something completely different. The whole thing feels natural, like the course was found rather than built.

If you come to Gulf Shores and you don't play Kiva, I don't know what you're doing. The other courses are good. Kiva is just on a different level in terms of feel and uniqueness. It's the course we'll lead with next time someone asks us where to play down here.

## Round 4: Craft Farms – Cypress Bend

![Craft Farms Cypress Bend](https://res.cloudinary.com/dak67vton/image/upload/f_auto,q_auto,w_1400/Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_3.42.24_PM_sddwkw.png)

Final round of the trip. 1:10 Saturday tee time at Craft Farms, an Arnold Palmer design and one of only two Palmer courses in Alabama (Cotton Creek is the sister course on the same property).

We hadn't done much research on this one going in, and that ended up being part of the fun. The front nine is solid. Playable, well-maintained, friendly off the tee, the kind of golf that lets you settle in without thinking too hard. We were tired by this point. Four rounds in 30 hours will do that.

And then the back nine shows up.

The back at Craft Farms is the reason you play this course. The 11th is a standout. Water all the way down the left side, a green that's tucked into a corner, everything well-manicured. The hole has a presence to it that the front doesn't. From there the back just keeps going. Better holes, more interesting layouts, water more in play, the kind of stretch where you're walking off greens going "okay, this is good."

If you played the front and walked off thinking it was just fine, you'd be wrong. You have to play the back. The back is the course.

We closed out the round, took the obligatory group photo on 18, and that was the trip. Two days, four courses, twelve hours of golf, and we were done.

## Things We'd Tell You

A few quick things for anyone thinking about putting this trip together:

- **Book through Tee Times USA.** Already said it, saying it again. The amount of friction it removed was the difference between this trip being a thing we executed and a thing we enjoyed.
- **Stay in Orange Beach.** The Summer House condo at Romar Beach worked. Big enough for four guys, close enough to all four courses.
- **Go three days, not two.** We did it in two and it worked, but it was a grind. A third day lets you spread the rounds out and not be wrecked by Saturday night.
- **Kiva first if you have one round.** If for some reason you're only playing one course down here, play Kiva. Not close.
- **Don't sleep on Craft Farms.** Front nine doesn't sell it. The back does. Stay for the back.
- **Fly into Pensacola.** Shorter drive than Mobile, and the cheaper flights tend to land there.

## So, Should You Go?

Yes. This was one of those trips where the expectations going in were modest and the trip outperformed them by a long way. The golf is great. The condo is great. The geography means you actually get to play golf instead of driving between courses. And the price point, without getting into specifics, is in a completely different universe than the bucket-list trips that get all the attention.

If you're a group of three or four trying to figure out where to go this year and you don't want to spend a year's salary on it, Gulf Shores is the move. We didn't expect to leave saying that, and we are. Already talking about going back, this time with another night and a fifth round.

If you want help putting it together, talk to Tee Times USA. They earned the recommendation.

That's the trip. Until the next one.

[social url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ylFf6p-KyAA" caption="Watch our Gulf Shores trip video" platform="yt"]
excerpt: Gulf Shores wasn't on our golf radar. Four rounds later, we're already planning to go back.
author: Cameron
